title: Why Text Differs from Images
concept: Unlike images with a fixed grid of pixel numbers, text is a sequence of discrete symbols with variable length and complex grammar, requiring tokenization before models can read it.

exercises.prompt: What is the main building block of text data?
exercises.explain: Images are represented by pixels, while text is represented by characters or words that need to be tokenized.

cards.title: Why Text is Special
cards.body: Have you ever wondered why AIs can easily read pictures of dogs but struggle to write a simple poem without making up words? It's because images and text are built completely differently! Let's explore why text is a whole different ballgame for computers.

cards.title: Pixels vs. Tokens
cards.body: An image is just a grid of numbers representing pixel colors. Text, however, is a sequence of discrete symbols (letters and words) with variable lengths and rules of grammar. Because computers don't naturally understand letters, text must be broken down into pieces called "tokens" before an AI can read it.

cards.title: The LEGO Train vs. Mosaic Puzzle
cards.body: Think of an image like a mosaic tile puzzle: every single tile is always in its fixed spot, and you just look at its color. Text is like a train of LEGO bricks. You can snap on as many or as few bricks as you want, they come in different shapes (words), and they must be connected in a specific order to make sense!

cards.title: Looking at a Cat vs. Reading "Cat"
cards.body: A computer looks at a photo of a cat and sees a 500x500 grid of color numbers. But if you give it the sentence "The cat sat," it doesn't see a grid. It breaks the sentence into tokens: ["The", " cat", " sat"], converting those words into numerical IDs to process them step-by-step.

cards.title: Is Text Easier Than Images?
cards.body: You might think that because letters are simple, text is easier for computers to read than complex colorful images. Actually, text is harder! Images have a predictable grid structure, while text has loose lengths, complex grammar, and hidden meanings that require extra translation steps.

cards.title: Grid vs. Sequence
cards.body: Compare how a computer views an image versus how it views a sentence.

left.text: A fixed grid of pixel numbers (e.g., 100x100 pixels).

right.text: A sequence of tokens with changing lengths and grammar.

exercises.prompt: What makes text data different from image data?

options.text: Text has variable length and complex grammar instead of a fixed grid.
Text is made of colored pixels, while images are made of words.
Text always has a fixed grid size of 256x256.
Text does not need to be converted to numbers for computers to read it.

exercises.explain: Unlike images, which are always neat grids of pixels, text can be any length (from a single word to a whole book) and must follow strict rules of grammar and meaning.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Computers can process sentences directly without converting the words into numbers first.
exercises.explain: Computers cannot read raw text directly. They must first convert the words into numbers (tokens) using a tokenizer.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of text processing.
exercises.sentence: Before a computer can read text, it must go through a process called _________ to break the words into smaller pieces.
exercises.explain: Because text consists of letters and words rather than a simple grid of numbers, it must undergo tokenization to turn the words into numbers the computer can understand.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs represents how a computer sees tokenized text?

options.text: A list of tokens: ["I", " love", " coding"]

exercises.explain: Only Option A shows a system outputting discrete tokens (words/symbols) representing text. Option B is an image-like grid of values, and Option C is just a simple state.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best summarizes why computers process text and images differently?

options.text: Images are made of discrete letters, while text is made of a fixed grid of color numbers.
Text and images are stored exactly the same way as grids of pixel numbers.
Images are structured grids of pixel numbers, while text is a sequence of symbols that needs tokenization.
Text has a fixed size and grid, whereas images have variable grammar and spelling.

exercises.explain: Images are regular grids of pixel values (numbers from 0 to 255) that always have a set size. Text is a sequence of discrete symbols (words/letters) of variable length that must be translated into tokens before a computer can read them.
